finding_text: A US visa expansion for Filipino nurses (2000-2006) caused 9 new nurses to be licensed in the Philippines for every 1 nurse who emigrated, increasing both the tertiary-educated nursing workforce and overall tertiary-educated labor stock. Net brain gain over 2000-2006: ~86,940 nurses (113,775 newly licensed minus 26,835 emigrants).
effect_size: ratio of 9 new licensed nurses per 1 emigrant nurse; 86,940 net new nurses over 7 years
method_used: Difference-in-differences exploiting exogenous US visa-cap expansion (Abarcar & Theoharides 2024 / cited as ref 20)
model_specification: Difference-in-differences (province × year × program-type) using pre/post US nurse-visa expansion and cross-province exposure to nursing-program supply. Effect-size is a ratio (newly licensed / emigrants), not a regression coefficient — effect_size_type left null.
scope_conditions: Philippines, 2000-2006; province × program-type × year panel; high training-supply elasticity from private nursing schools.

finding_text: When the US H-1B visa cap was relaxed (raising US wages of Indian IT workers by ~10%), IT employment in India rose by 5.8%, indicating induced computer-science skill acquisition exceeded the outflow.
effect_size: +10% Indian-IT US wages → +5.8% IT employment in India
method_used: Quasi-experimental general-equilibrium analysis exploiting H-1B cap variation (Khanna & Morales 2021 / ref 21)
model_specification: Structural / quasi-experimental general-equilibrium model linking US H-1B caps to US Indian wages and Indian IT employment. Reported magnitude is an elasticity (Δemployment%/Δwage%); no PAX enum slot for elasticity, so effect_size_type left null.
scope_conditions: India, 1990s-2000s; sector-year unit (IT sector specifically).

finding_text: Top academic achievers from five small/middle-income countries (Ghana, New Zealand, Tonga, Micronesia, Papua New Guinea) earn $35,000-$79,000 USD per year more after migrating, equivalent to a 53-600% income increase. The review reports a range across countries, not a point estimate.
effect_size: range +$35,000 to +$79,000/year; +53% to +600% income gain (across 5 countries)
method_used: Survey-based comparison of academic high achievers who migrated vs nonmigrants (Gibson & McKenzie 2012, ref 8)
model_specification: Cross-sectional comparison with selection adjustments using counterfactual home wages. Range is across 5 countries; no point estimate reported in review — effect_size_value left null to avoid hallucinated precision.
scope_conditions: Academic top achievers from 5 countries (Ghana, NZ, Tonga, Micronesia, Papua New Guinea).

finding_text: A one-standard-deviation increase in the post-2008-recession emigration rate of young, skilled Italians reduced new firm creation by 4.8%.
effect_size: -4.8% new firm creation per 1 SD increase in young skilled emigration
method_used: Shift-share IV exploiting post-2008 recession emigration shocks (Anelli et al. 2023 / ref 18)
model_specification: Shift-share IV with historical emigration shares × post-2008 emigration shocks; controls for local economic conditions
scope_conditions: Italy, post-2008 recession; unit is local labor market × year.

finding_text: Following EU enlargement (2004/2007), tertiary-educated emigration from Eastern European new-EU countries raised home-market labor costs by 7%. (From the origin-welfare perspective this is a cost-side adjustment, not a welfare gain.)
effect_size: +7% labor costs in origin countries
method_used: Staggered DID across origin-country × industry timing of EU labor-market access (Elsner et al. / ref 41)
model_specification: Staggered DID across countries-of-origin × industries × time of EU labor-market access
scope_conditions: Eastern European EU accession countries (post-2004); tertiary-educated emigrants only; short-run effects; unit is country × industry × year.

finding_text: Following EU enlargement, productivity in the home markets of Eastern European new-EU countries fell by 6% as tertiary-educated workers emigrated.
effect_size: -6% productivity in origin countries
method_used: Staggered DID across origin-country × industry timing of EU labor-market access (Elsner et al. / ref 41)
model_specification: Staggered DID across countries-of-origin × industries × time of EU labor-market access
scope_conditions: Eastern European EU accession countries; effects dissipate over time as firms adjust; unit is country × industry × year.

finding_text: A 10% increase in a country's high-skilled migrant ethnic network in the US (driven by 1990 US Immigration Act variation) raised manufacturing output in the sending country by 3% (elasticity ~0.30).
effect_size: elasticity 0.30 (manufacturing output w.r.t. ethnic network size)
method_used: DID exploiting 1990 US immigration reform variation across origin countries (Hovhannisyan & Keller / ref 37)
model_specification: DID with country-level treatment from 1990 US Immigration Act changes for scientists/engineers; reported as elasticity, not standardized beta — effect_size_type left null.
scope_conditions: Origin countries differentially affected by 1990 US scientist/engineer reform.

finding_text: A 1% increase in emigration from European countries (driven by EU mobility-law changes) increased patent applications in those origin countries by 0.64% over the subsequent two years (elasticity 0.64).
effect_size: elasticity 0.64 (patents w.r.t. emigration), 2-year lag
method_used: Quasi-experimental analysis exploiting European mobility-law changes (Prato 2024 / ref 46)
model_specification: Distributed-lag panel regression with policy-change instrument; reported as elasticity.
scope_conditions: European source countries with EU mobility-law variation.

finding_text: Returning Fulbright Fellows are cited 90% more in their home countries than a matched control group of nonmigrant scholars.
effect_size: +90% home-country citations vs control
method_used: Matched comparison of Fulbright recipients vs comparable nonrecipients (Kahn & MacGarvie 2016 / ref 33)
model_specification: Matched-comparison citation regression; effect is a percent difference vs control.
scope_conditions: Fulbright Fellows required to return to home country.

finding_text: Return migration of a US-trained African scientist increases the publication output of their nonmigrant scientist colleagues at home by 12% through improved knowledge access and networks.
effect_size: +12% colleague publication output per returning scientist
method_used: Quasi-experimental scholar-level analysis (Mohnen / ref 48)
model_specification: Scholar fixed-effects panel with returnee-arrival treatment; reported as percent change.
scope_conditions: African nonmigrant scientists working alongside US-trained returnees.

finding_text: Internationally mobile Chinese scholars had 7.3% more international collaborators than their nonmobile counterparts.
effect_size: +7.3% international collaborators
method_used: DID comparing mobile vs nonmobile Chinese scholars (ref 54)
model_specification: DID with scholar fixed effects and mobility treatment; effect is a percent difference.
scope_conditions: Chinese academic scholars; bibliometric coverage.

finding_text: Cross-border collaborations among researchers in new-EU countries fell after EU enlargement, as researchers with international linkages departed for other EU countries (qualitative DID, magnitude not numerically reported in review).
effect_size: qualitative decline (negative DID estimate)
method_used: DID across new-EU vs comparison researchers (ref 55)
model_specification: Researcher panel DID around EU enlargement
scope_conditions: Researchers in new-EU accession countries.

finding_text: Chinese provincial talent-return programs increased the valuation and productivity of resident firms whose corporate boards added returnee directors (staggered rollout DID; qualitative magnitudes in review).
effect_size: qualitative increase in firm valuation and productivity
method_used: Staggered DID exploiting timing of provincial return-migration incentive programs (ref 43)
model_specification: Staggered DID at firm level with provincial return-program treatment
scope_conditions: Chinese listed firms in provinces with return-migration incentive rollouts.

finding_text: Indian Fortune 500 employees randomly assigned (by HR rotation uncorrelated with their characteristics) to managers with US return-migrant experience filed more US patents than peers under non-returnee managers.
effect_size: qualitative positive effect on patent filings
method_used: Quasi-random HR assignment of employees to returnee vs non-returnee managers (Choudhury 2016 / ref 45)
model_specification: OLS with HR-rotation as as-if-random assignment
scope_conditions: Large Fortune 500 employer in India; specific patent-relevant business units.

finding_text: A randomized intervention to integrate Cape Verdean immigrants in Portugal had spillover effects on their closest contacts in Cape Verde, increasing support for gender equity in household decision-making by 4-6 percentage points.
effect_size: +4 to +6 pp support for gender equity
method_used: Randomized integration-intervention RCT with measured spillovers to nonmigrant contacts (ref 64)
model_specification: Randomized RCT with intent-to-treat estimates on nonmigrant contacts; reported as a 4-6 pp range across outcome variants — value left null to avoid hallucinated precision.
scope_conditions: Cape Verdean migrants in Portugal and their closest home-country contacts.

finding_text: The same Cape Verde RCT increased electoral participation among migrants' closest home-country contacts by 12 percentage points relative to contacts of untreated migrants.
effect_size: +12 pp electoral participation
method_used: Randomized integration-intervention RCT with measured spillovers (ref 64)
model_specification: Randomized RCT with ITT estimates on nonmigrant contacts; effect is a percentage-point change.
scope_conditions: Cape Verdean migrants in Portugal and their closest home-country contacts.

finding_text: Bangladeshi visa-lottery winners (won the right to work in Malaysia) had spouses 148% more likely to be identified as the household head than spouses of unsuccessful entrants.
effect_size: +148% likelihood female household head
method_used: Random-assignment visa lottery (Mobarak et al. / ref 65)
model_specification: ITT regression on lottery indicator with no controls (random assignment); effect is a relative-risk-style percent change.
scope_conditions: Bangladeshi households entering lottery for Malaysian work visas.

finding_text: Bangladeshi visa-lottery winners' households showed a 75% increase in females holding exclusive household decision-making authority, vs unsuccessful-entrant households.
effect_size: +75% exclusive female decision-making authority
method_used: Random-assignment visa lottery (Mobarak et al. / ref 65)
model_specification: ITT regression on lottery indicator (random assignment); effect is a relative-risk-style percent change.
scope_conditions: Bangladeshi households entering lottery for Malaysian work visas.

finding_text: Eliminating Filipina women's ability to work as entertainers in Japan reduced mean household income by 0.5% in Filipino provinces (moving from 25th to 75th percentile in pre-policy province dependence).
effect_size: -0.5% household income (25th→75th percentile dependence)
method_used: DID across Filipino provinces with differential pre-policy entertainer-employment dependence (ref 83)
model_specification: DID with province × year fixed effects, treatment intensity = pre-policy entertainer-employment dependence
scope_conditions: Philippines, post-2004 Japan entertainer-visa restrictions; unit is province-year.

finding_text: Eliminating Filipina entertainer migration to Japan raised the rate of child labor by 2.8 percentage points (25th→75th percentile dependence).
effect_size: +2.8 pp child labor rate (25th→75th percentile dependence)
method_used: DID across Filipino provinces with differential pre-policy entertainer-employment dependence (ref 83)
model_specification: DID with province × year fixed effects
scope_conditions: Philippines, post-2004 Japan entertainer-visa restrictions; unit is province-year. Direction 'positive' refers to child_labor_origin (i.e., emigration loss increases child labor).

finding_text: A one-standard-deviation positive remittance shock (driven by exchange-rate movements in migrant destinations) increased Philippine origin-province income per capita by 1,349 PHP (real 2010 pesos) and expenditure per capita by 1,224 PHP — each 0.12 SD increases.
effect_size: +1,349 PHP income/capita (~+0.12 SD); +1,224 PHP expenditure/capita (~+0.12 SD)
method_used: Shift-share IV using exchange-rate shocks at migrant destinations (Yang 2008 / ref 30)
model_specification: Shift-share IV with province-share × destination-exchange-rate shock
scope_conditions: Philippine provinces; period covered by FIES province-level data; unit is province-year.

finding_text: Across 53 African countries, IV-identified physician and nurse emigration rates do NOT lead to substantial reductions in domestic physician and nurse stocks (null finding).
effect_size: no significant effect on home physician/nurse stocks
method_used: Instrumental variables panel regression across 53 African countries (Bhargava et al. / ref 86)
model_specification: IV panel regression with destination-policy instruments for physician/nurse emigration
scope_conditions: 53 African countries; medical workforce only.

finding_text: The same 53-African-country IV study cannot detect any worsening of population health (infant mortality or disease prevalence) attributable to physician and nurse emigration (null finding).
effect_size: no significant effect on infant mortality or disease prevalence
method_used: IV panel regression across 53 African countries (Bhargava et al. / ref 86)
model_specification: IV panel regression with country and year fixed effects
scope_conditions: 53 African countries; aggregate population-health indicators.

finding_text: Mexican households' marginal propensity to spend remittance income on health care is approximately 6%.
effect_size: MPC = 0.06 on health care from remittance income
method_used: Mexican household-level analysis (Ambler et al. / ref 88)
model_specification: Household expenditure regression with instrumented remittance income; reported as marginal propensity to consume.
scope_conditions: Mexican migrant-sending households.

finding_text: A 1-percentage-point increase in the share of Mexican households containing a return migrant from the US led to a 13% decrease in the share of households without health-care access (SSIV using interior US enforcement variation as the instrument).
effect_size: +1 pp return-migrant share → -13% households without health-care access
method_used: Shift-share IV using cross-state variation in US interior immigration enforcement (ref 89)
model_specification: Shift-share IV with state interior-enforcement shocks × historical migration shares
scope_conditions: Mexican municipalities; period of US interior-enforcement variation; unit is municipality-year.

finding_text: The Nigerian government's random assignment of new doctors to rural communities (NYSC program) substantially reduced infant mortality and eliminated the rural-urban gap, indicating that within-country distribution of medical workers — not international emigration — is the binding constraint on rural population health.
effect_size: rural-urban infant mortality gap eliminated
method_used: Random assignment of doctors via NYSC program (Okeke / ref 87)
model_specification: ITT regression on random NYSC rural assignment
scope_conditions: Nigerian rural communities receiving NYSC doctor placements; unit is rural community.

finding_text: Migration-induced changes in social norms around reproductive health in the Philippines led to reduced origin-community fertility and lower infant mortality in shift-share IV analysis using destination reproductive-health policy variation.
effect_size: qualitative reduction in fertility and infant mortality
method_used: Shift-share IV using destination-country reproductive-health policy liberalization (ref 67)
model_specification: SSIV with province-share × destination-policy-shock
scope_conditions: Philippine provinces; reproductive-health norm transmission; unit is province-year.

finding_text: Sending international college students to study in high-quality democracies is associated with subsequent improvements in democratic quality in origin countries (cross-country panel association; suggestive but not causally identified).
effect_size: qualitative positive association
method_used: Cross-country panel analysis (Spilimbergo 2009 / ref 57)
model_specification: Cross-country panel regression with destination-democracy weights; suggestive non-experimental evidence.
scope_conditions: Cross-country; flows of students to high-quality democracies.

finding_text: Low-skilled migration of Malawians to South Africa benefited origin-area education and structural development in the long run, with returnee capital financing nonfarm investments and shifting rural workers from farming to nonfarm work.
effect_size: qualitative long-run positive education and structural-change effect
method_used: Causal panel evidence on Malawi-South Africa migration (Dinkelman & Mariotti / refs 29, 53)
model_specification: Panel regressions with cohort and area fixed effects; long-run outcomes 30+ years post-mine-employment-shock
scope_conditions: Malawian regions historically supplying mine labor to South Africa; long-run effects.

finding_text: Migration from Mexico to the US increased birth weights and reduced infant mortality in origin households, partly due to improved medical knowledge transmitted from migrants.
effect_size: qualitative increase in birth weights; qualitative reduction in infant mortality
method_used: Causal analysis of Mexico-US migration and health knowledge spillovers (ref 90)
model_specification: IV / panel design exploiting migration-network variation
scope_conditions: Mexican origin households with US migration exposure.

finding_text: Vietnamese refugee resettlement in the US created exogenous diaspora variation that increased bilateral US-Vietnam trade flows decades later — natural-experiment evidence that ethnic networks lower trade frictions.
effect_size: qualitative positive elasticity of bilateral trade w.r.t. refugee-driven diaspora
method_used: Natural experiment using Vietnamese refugee resettlement in US counties (Parsons & Vezina / ref 38)
model_specification: OLS with county-level refugee allocation as exogenous variation in diaspora stocks
scope_conditions: US counties × Vietnam pair; post-resettlement decades; unit is county-year-pair.

finding_text: Japanese ethnic networks formed during WWII internment causally increased post-war trade between US counties and Japan, providing further natural-experimental evidence on the migrant-network → trade channel.
effect_size: qualitative positive trade impact of historical network size
method_used: Natural experiment using WWII Japanese internment locations (ref 39)
model_specification: DID/IV with internment-camp locations as historical-network instrument
scope_conditions: US counties; post-WWII period; unit is county-year-pair.

finding_text: US counties with larger ancestry-based migrant networks send more foreign direct investment to migrants' origin countries (shift-share IV evidence on the diaspora→FDI channel).
effect_size: qualitative positive elasticity of FDI w.r.t. ancestry-network size
method_used: Shift-share IV on US-county ancestry data (Burchardi, Chaney, Hassan / ref 35)
model_specification: Shift-share IV using ancestry shares × push-factor shocks
scope_conditions: US counties × origin-country pair-year; ancestry-network IV variation.
